hero_summary: Kurd is one of the core maqamat in modern Arabic practice. It is not understood only from its scale, but from its melodic path, its points of repose, and its routes of coloring and return.
short_identity: A maqam built on Jins Kurd on Dukah (D), with a final repose on Dukah (D), a major upper center on Nawa (G), and a common upper coloring through the relation between the fourth and the sixth.

quick_facts.upper_jins: It usually expands around Jaharkah (F) and Nawa (G), with a frequent appearance of Nahawand on Jaharkah (F)
quick_facts.character: Clear, coherent, relatively pure in sound, and flexible in development
quick_facts.common_start: It usually begins by stabilizing Jins Kurd on Dukah (D) before opening the upper region

sayr.summary: Maqam Kurd usually begins from Dukah (D) with Jins Kurd clearly established: Dukah (D), Kurdish second (E-flat), Jaharkah (F), and Nawa (G). From this, the practical difference between it and Bayati becomes clear, because the second in Kurd is Kurd (E-flat), not Sikah (E half-flat). After the original jins is established, the maqam expands toward the fifth and above, where Nahawand on Jaharkah (F) may appear, or the relation between the fourth and the sixth may become strong in some phrases and instrumental passages, before it clearly returns to Dukah (D) in the final cadence.
resting_tones: Dukah (D): the tonic and natural final center of repose
Nawa (G): a very important secondary upper center
Kardan (upper C): a natural jawab station if the maqam expands into the upper region
motion_notes: Kurd is practically recognized by its lowered second: Kurd (E-flat)
The opening phrases should not be vague, because the maqam loses its identity quickly if its original jins is not stabilized
Some phrases may resemble Bayati or Nahawand, but the correct judgment depends on the final resting point and on the way the fifth and the upper region are treated
The relation between the fourth and the sixth is common in Kurd repertoire and affects the upper color of the maqam
common_path: Make clear the distinction between Kurd (E-flat) and Sikah (E half-flat)
Color the upper region through Nahawand on Jaharkah (F) or through the relation between the fourth and the sixth
Return clearly to Dukah (D) and cadence there

common_modulations: A common relation between Jaharkah (F) and the upper sixth in certain expansions and instrumental links
Some phrases may sound close to Bayati or Nahawand, but if the final return and cadence remain Kurdish, the function stays inside Kurd
A resemblance in the lower trunk alone is not enough; one must watch how the fifth is confirmed and how the upper region is treated

examples.title: 'Awwadt 'Ayni
examples.note: One of the most important twentieth-century examples of Maqam Kurd

examples.title: Ridak wa-Nnas Hawak
examples.note: An example of newer developments in Maqam Kurd in the mid-twentieth century

examples.title: Ba'id 'Annak
examples.note: An important example because the complete instrumental introduction begins in Maqam Kurd

reference_recordings.title: 'Awwadt 'Ayni
reference_recordings.note: A core reference for hearing Kurd in a mature and complete formulation

reference_recordings.title: Ridak wa-Nnas Hawak
reference_recordings.note: An important reference for the development of Kurd in the twentieth century

reference_recordings.title: Introduction to Ba'id 'Annak
reference_recordings.note: An important reference for hearing Kurd in instrumental introductions and links
